title: Working with the part that believes it will be abandoned if imperfect
author: Kalesh
excerpt: There's a part of you that believes something terrifying. It believes that if you mess up, if you show a flaw, if you're not good enough, people will leave. It...
metaDescription: There's a part of you that believes something terrifying. It believes that if you mess up, if you show a flaw, if you're not good enough, people will leav...
htmlContent:
There's a part of you that believes something terrifying. It believes that if you mess up, if you show a flaw, if you're not good enough, people will leave. It believes this so completely that your whole nervous system organizes around avoiding that outcome. You become careful. You become quiet. You become the person who triple-checks everything before sending an email, who rehearses conversations in the shower, who apologizes for things that aren't your fault. You become exhausting to yourself.

I know this part. I've sat with it in my own body for years. It's not stupid. It's not broken. It's a protector that learned a specific lesson very early, and it's been running the show ever since. Here's the thing. That lesson might have been true once. It might have been the only way to survive. But it's not true anymore. And your part doesn't know that yet.

The origin story of the perfectionist protector

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, talks about how our parts develop in response to real experiences. They're not abstract concepts. They're not metaphors. They're actual subpersonalities with their own feelings, beliefs, and jobs. The part that believes abandonment follows imperfection didn't just appear out of nowhere. It formed because at some point, you received a message that your worth was conditional.

Maybe it was a parent who withdrew love when you got a B instead of an A. Maybe it was a caregiver who criticized your appearance, your effort, your very existence. Maybe it was a teacher who praised you only when you performed perfectly, and you learned that approval was something you had to earn. Stay with me here. That part of you is not the problem. The problem is the system it's trying to survive inside.

Janina Fisher, who writes extensively about trauma and the fragmented self, describes how these protector parts operate. They're not trying to hurt you. They're trying to prevent the pain they know is coming. They remember what happened last time you made a mistake. They remember the cold silence, the sharp words, the feeling of being invisible. And they will do anything to stop that from happening again. Anything. Including making you feel like you're never enough.

Think about it. That voice in your head that says "you should have done better" is not your enemy. It's a terrified child who believes that your survival depends on being flawless. And it's been working overtime for years without a single day off. No wonder you're tired.

What happens when you try to silence this part

The wellness industry loves to tell you to "just let go" of perfectionism. To "love yourself unconditionally." To "embrace your imperfections." And those things sound nice. They sound like something a calm person in linen pants would say. But if you've ever tried to just let go of this part, you know what happens. It gets louder. It gets more desperate. It starts screaming at you in the middle of the night about that typo you made three years ago.

Here's the reality. You can't exorcise a part of yourself. You can't meditate it away. You can't positive-affirmation it into submission. That's not how parts work. That's not how the nervous system works. What you can do is learn to be with this part. To listen to it. To understand what it's so afraid of.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, taught us that trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story in your head. It's a physiological response. That tightness in your chest when you're about to submit something imperfect? That's your part. That knot in your stomach when you imagine someone being disappointed in you? That's your part. It's not a thought you can argue with. It's a whole-body experience.

So when you try to tell this part to shut up, you're not just arguing with a thought. You're fighting your own nervous system. And you will lose. Every time. Because that part is stronger than your willpower. It's been protecting you for decades. It knows how to win.

How to actually work with this part

The work is not about getting rid of the part. The work is about building a relationship with it. About showing it that you're an adult now. That you have resources. That you can handle disappointment. That you won't die if someone is upset with you. The part doesn't know this yet. It's still living in the past, still reacting to threats that no longer exist.

Start by noticing when the part shows up. Not judging it. Just noticing. "Oh, there's that part again. The one that believes I'll be abandoned if I'm imperfect." Notice where you feel it in your body. For me, it's a tight band across my ribs. For you, it might be a pressure in your throat or a hollow feeling in your stomach. Just notice. Don't try to change it. Don't try to fix it. Just be curious.

Then, talk to it. Not from your critical mind. From your compassionate self. Say something like, "I see you. I know you're scared. I know you're trying to protect me. Thank you for working so hard." This might feel ridiculous at first. It might feel fake. Do it anyway. The part needs to hear that you're not trying to get rid of it. That you actually appreciate what it's been doing.

And then ask it what it needs. Not what it wants from the world. What it needs from you. Does it need reassurance that you'll be okay if someone is disappointed? Does it need to know that you have other resources now? Does it need to feel your adult presence, your groundedness, your ability to handle discomfort? Wild, right? The part that's been controlling your life might just need you to show up for it.

Building the capacity to disappoint others

This is the hard part. This is where the rubber meets the road. Because eventually, you have to test the part's belief. You have to be imperfect on purpose. You have to let someone down. You have to see what actually happens.

I'm not saying you should be careless or cruel. I'm saying you should practice being human. Send an email with a typo. Show up five minutes late to a casual coffee date. Say no to something you don't want to do. Admit that you don't know the answer. Watch what happens. Nine times out of ten, nothing terrible happens. The world doesn't end. People don't leave. And your part gets a tiny piece of evidence that maybe, just maybe, it's wrong.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," emphasizes that healing requires new experiences. Not just understanding. Not just insight. Actual lived experiences that contradict the old beliefs. Your part needs to feel, in its body, that imperfection does not equal abandonment. It needs to experience the safety of being seen and accepted even when you're not perfect.

This is terrifying. I won't pretend it isn't. Every time I do this, my part screams at me. It tells me I'm being reckless. It tells me I'm going to lose everything. But I do it anyway. Because I know that the only way out is through. And I know that my part deserves to rest. It's been working so hard for so long. It deserves to know that it can relax.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Because part of this work is learning that you can say no, that you can disappoint people, and that your worth is not determined by their reaction. Boundaries are how you show your part that you're safe now.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Many of us developed this part because our emotional needs weren't met as children. We learned to perform for love. We learned that being good enough was the price of connection. This book can help you understand where that pattern came from.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). If your part developed because you had to be the responsible one, the perfect one, the one who held everything together, this book will help you see how that dynamic shaped you.

What recovery looks like in real life

Recovery from this part doesn't mean you never feel the fear of abandonment again. It doesn't mean you suddenly become a carefree person who never checks their work. That's not realistic. That's not even desirable. Your part has skills. It's meticulous. It's careful. It's attuned to social dynamics. Those are gifts, not problems.

Recovery means the part is no longer in charge. It means you can hear its voice without obeying it. It means you can make a mistake and not spiral into a shame attack. It means you can disappoint someone and still know that you are okay. It means the part becomes a trusted advisor instead of a tyrannical boss.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work is foundational for understanding how these parts form and how to work with them compassionately.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your part relax a little? Did it tense up, waiting for the catch? That's okay. That's normal. That's just your part doing its job. You don't have to fix it right now. You just have to notice it.

The truth is, I don't know if my part will ever fully believe that it's safe. I don't know if yours will either. But I know that every time I sit with it, every time I show it compassion instead of criticism, every time I take a small risk and survive, the part gets a little quieter. A little softer. A little more willing to trust me.

And maybe that's enough. Maybe the goal isn't to be free of the part. Maybe the goal is to be in relationship with it. To hold its fear without being controlled by it. To let it speak without letting it drive. To be the adult that the part never had.

You are that adult now. You have resources. You have support. You have a nervous system that can learn new patterns. Your part doesn't know this yet. But you can show it. One imperfect moment at a time.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to setting boundaries and protecting your energy
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Understanding and healing from childhood emotional neglect
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Validating and understanding the impact of growing up with emotionally unavailable parents
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - A guide to working with trauma-related parts using Internal Family Systems and neuroscience
